evidence: Ray's advice—'If you only have one, don't expect to make any money'—reveals that acquisition value is created primarily by competition, not by the intrinsic value of what's being sold.
In M&A, you're not selling a company. You're selling the fear of losing it to a competitor. The product is FOMO, not technology.

source_display: The Earnout Trap as Risk Transfer
evidence: Earnouts are theoretically 'aligned incentives'—founders do well if the acquisition succeeds. But Ray's experience shows acquirers can manipulate success metrics, making earnouts a one-sided bet.

source_display: The Earnout Trap as Risk Transfer
evidence: What looks like incentive alignment is often risk transfer. When the party defining success metrics is the same party measuring them, 'alignment' becomes a polite word for 'control.'

Ilse was building to a higher standard than needed (30-day stories) until V clarified that acquirer demos have lower requirements. The gap between what engineers assume and what's actually needed for business goals is a leading indicator of communication health.
Ilse spoke with Gunderson (lawyer). If cash payout, need to minimize tax exposure. Decision (previously cleared with V): Build it right instead of quick temporary demo.
Kathy Pham: VP/Head of AI at Workday (Logan relationship)

source_display: Better at Job = Worse at Interviewing Paradox
evidence: The better you are at a job, the worse you are at interviewing because you've been fucking employed, because people have not wanted to let you go.

source_display: Better at Job = Worse at Interviewing Paradox
evidence: Interview skill and job skill are inversely correlated at the high end. We've built a system that selects for people who are good at *leaving* jobs, not *doing* them.
You're essentially reallocating a lot of low grade assets that are filtering and vetting your candidates to maybe a smaller number of higher grade employees that are more so engaging...
Most companies allocate their lowest-value cognitive resources (junior recruiters, screening tools) to their highest-stakes decisions (who joins the team).

source_display: The Story Premium - Information Asymmetry Through Narrative
evidence: Candidates who can articulate their experience as coherent stories get dramatically better scores. A candidate with 6 rich stories beats a candidate with 20 years of undifferentiated experience.
We're not actually measuring competence — we're measuring narrative fluency. The story *is* the product. Coaching candidates on story development might have 10x the ROI of skill development.
V demonstrated Careerspan's capabilities to Shivam. Schedule a follow-up call with V's head of AI to discuss implementation details. Meet in Brooklyn on Wednesday before 8pm event.
Goal: Build a candidate intelligence graph to match incoming requirements to their engineer network. They have ~100 AI engineering resumes/profiles already.
Has supply side (engineer database) but needs intelligence layer. Wants filterable, configurable intelligence layer on candidates.
